notes: ONE live aider REPL turn whose assistant reply lands as MULTIPLE partial flushes before the turn-end signal; the whole streamed turn must be a SINGLE contiguous working→ready arc with NO premature flicker to ready before end-of-turn. AIDER'S 'PARTIAL FLUSH' UNIT: aider has no token-level JSONL streaming event — the markdown history (.aider.chat.history.md) is written as completed message blocks, and its incremental token rendering is a terminal-UI concern that never reaches the file. The aider analog of a partial flush is the per-MODEL-CALL prose block terminated by its own `> Tokens: …` line: under --yes-always a single `####` user turn can drive several model calls (an initial reply, auto-accepted file-adds, a follow-up analysis), each closed by a `> Tokens:` line (see replaydata/agents/aider/regression/yes-always-multi-step/transcript.md — two prose blocks within one #### turn). WHY THE TURN STAYS WORKING ACROSS FLUSHES: closeModelCall (parser.go:153-188) emits assistant_message — NOT turn_done — at each `> Tokens:` line and leaves turnOpen=true (TestParser_MultiModelCall_KeepsTurnOpen), and IsAgentDone (session.go:461-496) does NOT treat aider's assistant_message as done, so no per-call flush can flip the session to ready. The session therefore holds working across every partial flush — the no-flicker invariant the scenario pins. The streaming_window invariant 'no state_transition to ready' over the working span forbids any intermediate ready flap; 'no state_transition to waiting' forbids any waiting transition. WHY INTERACTIVE (not headless `aider --message`): headless aider is single-shot and exits the instant it emits the final reply, so the daemon's scanner can miss the working→ready settle and the teardown before process_exited; the interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh tmux REPL) keeps aider alive across the streamed turn so the settle is observed cleanly, then a single tmux teardown yields one clean process_exited. AIDER TURN-END IS SYNTHESIZED, NOT MARKED: aider's only clean end-of-turn signal is IdleFlush, which synthesizes turn_done after aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter=1500ms of transcript quiet (parser.go:54,216-229), surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector working-refresh poll (~5s effective latency) — so the recipe ends on a generous trailing sleep (≥10s) to let that flip fire AND be observed before teardown (a teardown race bit a sibling cell at 8s). SCOPE_NOTE (the observes:partial carve-out — THE DOCUMENTED RISK THIS SPEC TARGETS AS THE NO-FLICKER IDEAL): because end-of-turn is synthesized purely from 1500ms of file quiet, a SLOW remote model call that leaves the history file quiet for >1500ms BETWEEN flushes (mid-turn) can make IdleFlush synthesize a PREMATURE turn_done → a premature working→ready → exactly the flicker this scenario forbids. The no-flicker property therefore holds for normal back-to-back flushes (the common case) but is NOT airtight against a slow remote call mid-turn; this spec is the DOCUMENTED TARGET — the no-flicker ideal — and the streaming_window invariant is what a future recording is validated against to surface any such premature-ready drift. AIDER IS FilesUnderCWD: the daemon keys the session by proc-<PID> (no transcript UUID rollout session); the whole arc stays on the one proc-<PID> row. LAZY TRANSCRIPT: .aider.chat.history.md materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) before the per-cwd transcript appears — session_birth, which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s of launch', legitimately matches that pre-session. The TURN ARC, by contrast, must land on the session that goes working: turn_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working in the whole recording (the pre-session never enters working); turn_end, pid_bind, and teardown then pin to turn_start via same_session_as so a co-occurring pre-session can never satisfy them. HOW runs differ: exact assistant wording, the exact NUMBER of partial flushes / model calls, per-call token counts, and sub-second timing vary (model-output variance); stable structurally — a single sustained working span across all flushes, no waiting, one working→ready settle, one process_exited, final ready. NOTE: this spec is the DOCUMENTED TARGET — validated when aider auth is available in-env (no provider key present at authoring time; headless aider hangs on OpenRouter OAuth). Single proc-<PID> session, one process_exited.

text: A session appears in ready within 2s of REPL launch (the proc-<PID> pre-session; the lazy transcript has not materialized yet)

User prompt for a long multi-part reply → working (binds the live aider session; the pre-session never enters working). The streaming window opens here: state stays working across every partial flush — no flicker to ready before the turn-end signal arrives

During the streamed reply the session stays solidly in working across multiple partial flushes (each per-model-call prose block closed by its own `> Tokens:` line emits assistant_message, not turn_done); none must promote to ready, and state never enters waiting
Only the synthesized end-of-turn (IdleFlush after the history goes quiet) settles working→ready; despite the multiple streamed flushes the turn is one contiguous arc and state never entered waiting
PID bound to the live aider session at birth (aider binds PID at process launch, before the turn; anchored to recording start, still pinned to the working session via same_session_as)
REPL torn down once after the streamed turn → a single process_exited on the session; state immediately before teardown is ready
